subject: Science
question_text:
Ahmad brought five objects to Science class and tested each one with a bar magnet: a steel paper clip, a wooden pencil, a copper coin, an iron nail, and a rubber eraser.

Which two objects would be attracted to the magnet?
options: The steel paper clip and the copper coin would be attracted because both are made of shiny metals, and all shiny metals are magnetic.
The wooden pencil and the rubber eraser would be attracted because they are very lightweight, and magnets pull light objects more easily than heavy ones.
The steel paper clip and the iron nail would be attracted because steel contains iron, and iron and steel are both magnetic materials.
All five objects would be attracted to some degree, because every material in the world responds to a strong enough magnet.
worked_solution:
Step 1: Recall which materials are magnetic.
  Magnetic materials: iron, steel (contains iron), nickel, cobalt.
  Non-magnetic: wood, rubber, plastic, glass, copper, aluminium.
Step 2: Test each object:
  Steel paper clip → steel contains iron → MAGNETIC ✓
  Wooden pencil → wood → NOT magnetic
  Copper coin → copper → NOT magnetic (even though it is a metal)
  Iron nail → iron → MAGNETIC ✓
  Rubber eraser → rubber → NOT magnetic

The answer is C.

wrong_explanations.A: Not all metals are magnetic. Copper is a metal but it is NOT attracted to a magnet. Only IRON-CONTAINING metals (iron, steel, nickel, cobalt) are magnetic. Shininess has nothing to do with magnetism — a shiny copper coin is not magnetic, while a dull grey iron nail is.
wrong_explanations.B: Magnetism has nothing to do with weight. A heavy iron block is strongly attracted to a magnet, while a light copper coin (similar weight to a paper clip) is not attracted at all. Magnetic attraction depends on the MATERIAL, not the weight.
wrong_explanations.D: Not all materials are magnetic. Wood, rubber, plastic, copper, aluminium, and glass are non-magnetic — no magnet, no matter how strong, will attract them. Magnetic force only acts on iron, steel, nickel, and cobalt.

examiner_note: Magnetic materials: iron, steel, nickel, cobalt. All others are non-magnetic. A common PSLE trap is to say 'all metals are magnetic' — this is WRONG. Copper and aluminium are metals but are non-magnetic. Always check: does it contain iron?

subject: Science
question_text:
During a Science experiment, Siti had two bar magnets labelled X and Y. When she pushed the North pole of magnet X towards the North pole of magnet Y, the magnets pushed each other apart.

She then turned magnet Y around and pushed the North pole of magnet X towards the South pole of magnet Y. What would she observe this time?
options: The magnets would push each other apart again, because bar magnets always repel any other bar magnet regardless of which ends are facing each other.
The magnets would pull towards each other and attract, because unlike poles (North facing South) attract each other.
The magnets would attract each other weakly, because turning a magnet around reduces its magnetic strength by half.
The magnets would have no effect on each other, because once two magnets have repelled each other in the first test, they permanently lose their magnetic properties.
worked_solution:
Step 1: Recall the rules for magnetic poles.
  LIKE poles (N-N or S-S) → REPEL each other.
  UNLIKE poles (N-S or S-N) → ATTRACT each other.
Step 2: First test — N pole of X facing N pole of Y → LIKE poles → REPEL (pushed apart). ✓
Step 3: Second test — N pole of X facing S pole of Y → UNLIKE poles → ATTRACT.

The magnets would attract and pull towards each other.

The answer is B.

wrong_explanations.A: Magnets do NOT always repel each other. The behaviour depends on which poles are facing each other. Like poles (N-N or S-S) repel. Unlike poles (N-S) attract. Turning magnet Y around changes which pole faces magnet X, changing the interaction from repulsion to attraction.
wrong_explanations.C: Turning a magnet around does not change its strength — it simply presents a different pole to the other magnet. The magnetic force is the same strength as before; only the direction of the force changes (from repulsion to attraction) because the poles facing each other are now unlike.
wrong_explanations.D: Repelling another magnet does not damage or demagnetise a magnet. Magnets retain their magnetic properties after any number of interactions with other magnets. Demagnetisation happens through heating, dropping, or hammering — not through magnetic repulsion.

examiner_note: Always remember: Like poles repel, Unlike poles attract. In PSLE diagrams, N and S poles are clearly labelled. Identify what poles are facing, then apply the rule. A useful memory trick: 'U attract' — Unlike poles Attract.
